literal: person, man, the Person; in Vedic usage often the cosmic Person (Puruṣa-Sūkta, Ṛgveda 10.90)
invariant_definition: *Puruṣa* is the conscious pole contrasted with the material-causal order. In the strict Sāṃkhya-Yoga register, this means the witness that does not act, change, or evolve, though agency and enjoyment are imputed to it through conjunction with *prakṛti*, *buddhi*, and *ahaṅkāra*. That register is foundational for later Vedānta even where it is rejected or reworked. Hence the term moves across three linked uses: the Vedic cosmic Person, the Sāṃkhya plurality of indifferent witnesses, and the Vedāntic supreme Person in expressions such as *puruṣottama*. What matters philosophically is that *puruṣa* is not merely 'person' in a modern moral or juridical sense. It names the conscious principle as distinct from the body-mind complex. The main disputes concern whether *puruṣa*s are many or one, whether witnesshood exhausts the conscious principle, and whether the supreme reality is best described impersonally as consciousness itself or personally as the highest *puruṣa*. Even schools that identify *puruṣa* with Bhagavān continue to define bondage through a confusion between the conscious principle and the *prakṛti* apparatus.

school_framing.shared_core: *Puruṣa* names **the conscious witnessing pole that stands uncombined with material change, for whose sake *prakṛti* operates** (*Sāṃkhya-Kārikā* 17–19; YS 1.16, 2.20). All schools that retain the term agree on this witness-structure. Vedāntic schools then disagree on whether *puruṣa* is plural (Sāṃkhya, Yoga, Tattva-vāda, Viśiṣṭādvaita) or one with *brahman* (Advaita).
school_framing.register_axes_note: **Real disagreement** on plurality of *puruṣa*s (Sāṃkhya/Yoga: many; Advaita: *eka*) — same M+E register, same scope-inside, opposing claims (AF1). **Same concept, different aspect** when Vaiṣṇava schools use *Puruṣottama* in a saviśeṣa M+AD register while Sāṃkhya keeps the term strictly Φ+E.

per_school.definition: In Advaita, the śruti-expression `hṛdyantarjyotiḥ puruṣaḥ` is read by `lakṣaṇā`: once the `tvam`-padārtha is freed from `upādhi`, `puruṣa` denotes not the empirical `kartṛ-bhoktṛ` but `sākṣi-caitanya` alone [[SB 10](cite://madhusudana/siddhanta-bindu/10)]. The many jīvas are therefore only `avidyā`-kalpita delimitations of that one self, so the highest sense of `puruṣa` is the non-dual ātman rather than a plurality of ultimately distinct conscious subjects [[MK 3.13](cite://gaudapada/mandukya-karika/3.13)][[MK 3.14](cite://gaudapada/mandukya-karika/3.14)].

Puruṣottama names Nārāyaṇa, the `parabrahman` of endless `kalyāṇa-guṇa`s who stands as `antaryāmin` to the whole `cid-acid` body and is therefore denoted by the personal register of `puruṣa` in its eminent sense [[VS §6](cite://ramanuja/vedartha-samgraha/§6)][[ŚrīBh 6](cite://ramanuja/shri-bhasya/6)][[YCs 1](cite://yamuna/catuh-shloki/1)]. The finite self remains a real `pratyagātman` distinct in each `kṣetra`, while the ordered whole of selves and matter is governed by one `eka-pradhāna-puruṣa` whose feet are the proper object of `dhyāna`, `arcana`, and `praṇāma` [[ĀS 3](cite://yamuna/atma-siddhi/3)][[ĪS 16-18](cite://yamuna/siddhi-trayam/16-18)].

Madhva reads scriptural `puruṣa`-language hierarchically: `kṣara` designates perishable beings, `akṣara` the imperishable selves, and beyond both stands the unique `Paramātman` proclaimed as `Puruṣottama` [[Gītā 15.16](cite://madhva/mayavada-khandana/15.16)][[Gītā 15.17](cite://madhva/mayavada-khandana/15.17)][[Gītā 15.18](cite://madhva/mayavada-khandana/15.18)]. The jīva is therefore never `svarūpataḥ` identical with the Lord: the unsurpassed `puruṣa` is Viṣṇu alone, the supreme terminus beyond the `avyakta` and every dependent self [[Kaṭha 1.3.11](cite://madhva/mayavada-khandana/Upaniṣad 1.3.11)][[BBsBh 1.1.1.7](cite://madhva/brahma-sutra-bhasya/1.1.1.7)].

In Gauḍīya usage, `puruṣottama` names Bhagavān as the personal object of `ahaitukī` and `avyavahitā` bhakti, so the term is received within `bhajana` as the highest person toward whom devotion is uninterruptedly directed [[BRS 1.1.13](cite://rupa-gosvami/bhakti-rasamrta-sindhu/1.1.13)]. The school's theological use of the word accordingly preserves its full personal force within `sevā` rather than thinning it into a merely impersonal consciousness-term [[BRS 1.1.13](cite://rupa-gosvami/bhakti-rasamrta-sindhu/1.1.13)].

For Nimbārka's line, the jīva is Śrī Puruṣottama's `aṃśa`, neither `atyantabhinna` nor `atyantābhinna`, because `aṃśatva` is construed as `śakti` rather than as a severed material part [[BS 2.3.42](cite://srinivasa/vedanta-kaustubha/2.3.42)]. The highest sense of `puruṣa` therefore belongs to the Lord whose plenitude grounds the dependent being of the many selves without canceling their own real standing [[ChU 3.12.6](cite://nimbarka/vedanta-parijata-saurabha/Upaniṣad 3.12.6)].

In Śuddhādvaita, `puruṣottama` denotes the concrete Brahman whose attainment is the telos of `brahmajñāna`: the removal of `avidyā` gives the soul `svarūpa-yogyatā` for relation to the Lord [[BSB 1.1.12-19](cite://vallabha/anu-bhasya/1.1.12-19)]. That attainment is completed through `svīyatva` and `bhakti-bhāva`, so `puruṣa` retains its fully personal force within grace-governed participation in Bhagavān [[BSB 1.1.12-19](cite://vallabha/anu-bhasya/1.1.12-19)].

In Sāṃkhya, *puruṣa* is established as a real principle because the composite exists for another, because experience requires a subject, and because the striving for *kaivalya* would otherwise be unintelligible [[SK 17](cite://isvarakrsna/samkhya-karika/17)]. Puruṣa-s are many, and each is characterized as witness, neutral, seer, and non-agent, precisely in contrast to the *traiguṇya* structure of *prakṛti* [[SK 18](cite://isvarakrsna/samkhya-karika/18)][[SK 19](cite://isvarakrsna/samkhya-karika/19)]. Agency and world-process belong only by conjunction: although the guṇa-s act, the indifferent *puruṣa* appears as if agent, and creation proceeds for the sake of its seeing, until it stands apart like a spectator before a now-cessant *prakṛti* [[SK 20](cite://isvarakrsna/samkhya-karika/20)][[SK 21](cite://isvarakrsna/samkhya-karika/21)][[SK 65](cite://isvarakrsna/samkhya-karika/65)].

Yoga inherits the Sāṃkhya `puruṣa` as the conscious pole disclosed by `viveka`, but it adds Īśvara as a `puruṣa-viśeṣa` untouched by `kleśa`, `karma`, `vipāka`, and `āśaya` [[YV 1.24](cite://vijnanabhiksu/yoga-varttika/1.24)]. The contemplative function of `puruṣa` is thus preserved while `īśvara-praṇidhāna` gives practice a determinate personal orientation within the same ontology [[YV 1.23](cite://vijnanabhiksu/yoga-varttika/1.23)][[YV 1.24](cite://vijnanabhiksu/yoga-varttika/1.24)].

In Nyāya's causal-theological discussion, `puruṣa` is the karma-bearing agent whose acts do not by themselves account for the observed arising of fruits, and this asymmetry is one of the grounds on which Īśvara is inferred as cause [[NS 4.1.19](cite://aksapada/nyaya-sutra/4.1.19)][[NS 4.1.21](cite://aksapada/nyaya-sutra/4.1.21)]. The term therefore functions within the analysis of agency, fruition, and ordered moral causality, with `puruṣa-karma` and its non-autonomous `phala` explicitly at issue [[NS 4.1.20](cite://aksapada/nyaya-sutra/4.1.20)][[NS 4.1.21](cite://aksapada/nyaya-sutra/4.1.21)].

Śabara defines the `codanā`-lakṣaṇa `artha` as that which connects the `puruṣa` with `niḥśreyasa`, so `puruṣa` enters the system as the qualified ritual subject upon whom Vedic injunction works [[MS 1.1.2 / ŚBh ad loc.](cite://jaimini/mimamsa-sutra/1.1.2)]. The school accordingly binds `puruṣa` to `bhāvanā`, `adhikāra`, and the efficacy of `dharma`: what matters is the agent brought into relation with the `śreyaskara` artha by `apauruṣeya` śabda [[MS 1.1.2 / ŚBh ad loc.](cite://jaimini/mimamsa-sutra/1.1.2)].

Mādhyamaka allows `puruṣa` only as a conventional designation within dependent discourse; the `māyāpuruṣa` image shows that apparent agent and negation can function without any `svabhāva`-bearing self [[VV 23](cite://nagarjuna/vigraha-vyavartani/23)]. What appears as personhood is therefore usable in `vyavahāra` while empty in `paramārtha`, exactly like the magical apparition invoked in the example [[VV 22](cite://nagarjuna/vigraha-vyavartani/22)][[VV 23](cite://nagarjuna/vigraha-vyavartani/23)].

Yogācāra deploys `puruṣa`-language only at the level of represented content: dreams and preta-experience can present women and men without any external object, so the appearance of persons proves nothing about an enduring witness outside `vijñapti` [[Vvs 3](cite://vasubandhu/vimsatika/and Preta Replies)]. The deeper gain of the analysis is entry into `pudgala-nairātmya`, since from seed and appearance the sixfold consciousness proceeds, but no single `draṣṭṛ` or `mantṛ` is found there [[Vvs 9](cite://vasubandhu/vimsatika/9 and Entry into *Pudgala-Nairātmya*)].

In Pratyabhijñā/Trika, the bound subject is not an ultimately independent witness called *puruṣa* over against a separate *prakṛti*; rather, the transmigrating condition is Śiva-consciousness under self-contraction, mala, and māyic limitation [[PH 9](cite://ksemaraja/pratyabhijna-hrdayam/9)][[PS 36](cite://abhinavagupta/paramartha-sara/36)]. Even in that contracted state, the subject continues to perform the five divine acts, so liberation comes through recognition that inward-turning cognition is itself *citi* and not through the isolation of an inert spectator [[PH 10](cite://ksemaraja/pratyabhijna-hrdayam/10)][[PH 13](cite://ksemaraja/pratyabhijna-hrdayam/13)]. Abhinavagupta accordingly distinguishes the dehāntarālaga *puruṣa* of transmigratory continuity from the *paraḥ puruṣaḥ* who remains untouched by the modifications of māyā [[PS 92](cite://abhinavagupta/paramartha-sara/92)][[PS 36](cite://abhinavagupta/paramartha-sara/36)].

In Jainism, the functional analogue to *puruṣa* is *jīva* rather than a preferred *puruṣa*-vocabulary: the soul is defined by *upayoga* and can exist in either the worldly or liberated condition [[TS 2.8](cite://umasvati/tattvartha-sutra/2.8)][[TS 2.10](cite://umasvati/tattvartha-sutra/2.10)]. Bondage occurs when the passion-marked *jīva* appropriates karmically fit *pudgala*, and the doctrine accordingly treats a plurality of souls as standing in mutual relation rather than collapsing into a single cosmic subject [[TS 8.2](cite://umasvati/tattvartha-sutra/8.2)][[TS 5.21](cite://umasvati/tattvartha-sutra/5.21)].

translator_note: 'Person', 'man', and 'spirit' all mislead if used without qualification. Modern English 'person' carries moral-legal and post-Lockean baggage; 'spirit' imports Christian and idealist associations. In Sāṃkhya, *puruṣa* is the witness distinct from the apparatus of action; in Vaiṣṇava and Vedāntic usage, it often names the supreme Person rather than the finite individual. The term should therefore be left untranslated unless the register is made explicit: witness-consciousness, cosmic Person, or supreme Person.
